author: @SUF.YAN_JutT
text:
Great breakdown! I watched the full video and I'm really impressed by how well the Exynos 2600 performed on the international Galaxy S26 unit you tested. For years, Exynos chips were the weak link of Samsung's lineup, but this time it genuinely seems like Samsung has cracked the code.

A few highlights that stood out to me from the performance section:

The S26 (Exynos 2600) destroyed multi-core scores in Geekbench 6, beating even the A19.

In the Lightroom export of 99 photos, the Galaxy came in first — ahead of the iPhone.

In CapCut 4K rendering, the Galaxy finished in just 15 seconds, which is insane.

In 3DMark Wildlife Extreme, the Galaxy topped the best loop score.
My one big request: please do a dedicated full deep-dive video on the Exynos 2600! I'd love to see extended gaming tests (BGMI, Genshin Impact, Wuthering Waves), sustained performance over 30+ minute sessions, thermal throttling charts, per-hour battery drain, and a long-term 2–3 month follow-up on how it holds up over time. This matters a lot for international buyers like me who will never get the Snapdragon variant.
Based on this video, it genuinely feels like the international S26 is no longer a "worse" option. That's a huge deal. Would love a separate Exynos 2600 dedicated test to confirm this once and for all!

author: @renovatio6971
text:
Samsung keeps innovating year after year, pushing the smartphone industry forward, while many Apple-focused YouTubers continue to downplay those achievements and shamelessly overhype every new iPhone. Ironically, that constant bias has only pushed Samsung to go even harder, driving them to keep delivering real innovation—and the results are clearly visible.

At this point, many flagship Android phones easily compete with—or even outperform—the iPhone in several areas. Yet the same reviewers still act as if Apple is always ahead. Biased reviews might protect Apple’s image for a while, but they can’t change reality. Eventually people start noticing the gap between hype and actual innovation—and we’re already beginning to see that shift now. It's good to see and hear it from you - the tone of that conversation is starting to change.
